text: followed him, stopping to stare up at the house on the other side. It was a huge, dark shape, its upper floors — as though the second story weren’t enough, there was a third-floor window wedged under the peak of the roof — bathed in the gentle glow of the nearest lamp. Her shack allowed a couple feet of clearance for her head. How much did this place have ? How much did a person need ? For that matter, how much did a bot need ? Ronin went up the steps to the front door, where he fished something out of his pocket. He fiddled with the handle for a few seconds before swinging the door open. Without a word, he went inside . Lara mounted the steps, freezing at the top. The impenetrable darkness beyond the doorway was not welcoming . “This is it,” he said from within. It sounded like he tossed his pack on the floor. “You coming in ?” “I can’t see .” “You can’t. Just a moment.” His boots thumped across the floor. There was a soft click . Lara turned away from the sudden flare of light, shielding her eyes with her arm. When her vision finally adjusted, she looked inside, mouth agape . The lights on the ceiling filled the place with bright, pure light, putting her little lantern to shame. She entered slowly. This first room was big enough to fit her shack three times over within its pristine white walls. On the left, a set of stairs led to the next level, and straight ahead was an opening into another room. Even the floor seemed to glow; it appeared to be made of wooden boards, but she’d never seen wood so smooth and shiny . Ronin walked past her and closed the door, locking it. Lara went to it after he moved away, tugging on the handle, tapping on its front. Solid and secure . She’d only walked a mile, but had entered an entirely different world . “There are bedrooms upstairs, if you’d like to select one .” “ Bedrooms ?” “Rooms with beds in them.” He gestured to the stairs . Lara moved to their base and stared up. More thick darkness concealed the top. “I just… go up ?” “Do you want me to go first?” Ronin asked. She glanced at him. Was that the hint of a smile on his face ? “Is there something up there I should worry about ?” He reached past her and flicked another switch. Light obliterated the darkness above . “ No .” “Okay.” Lara took a deep breath and set her foot on the first step, cringing at the squish of water from her boot . “You can take them off, if you want .” She didn’t need to be told twice. Tugging them off, she dropped them on the floor beside the first step. Her feet were pale, skin wrinkled, and the floor beneath them was cold. She climbed onto the bottom step and looked down. The material on the stairs was different; soft and dry and cushioned, with little fibers that slipped between her toes . Continuing to the top, she stopped at the landing; the dim hallway ran to either side. She located another of the little switches, and, tentatively, flicked it up. Light filled the hall . There was a door at each end, with two more on the wall opposite the stairs and another to her left. A place like this could house ten or fifteen people, at least. Why would anyone — especially a bot — ever need so much space to themselves ? She turned left, opening the door at the end of the hall, and felt around on the wall until she clicked on the light. This was another large room. A huge bed stood against the far wall, draped in a thick blanket, a decorative wooden panel at its head and foot. The thing was large enough for three adults to sleep in comfortably . She walked to it and ran her hand over the blanket, marveling at the soft material. When she leaned down, the bed sank in slightly, yielding a bit to her weight. It was about as different from her hard pallet as she could have imagined . Another piece of furniture stood near the door. As tall as her chest, it was crafted of dark wood, its face comprised of five wide drawers. She opened them one at a time, finding them all empty . Her curiosity brought her to the swaths of cloth hanging on the wall beside the bed. There was a window behind them, blocked by thin, flimsy plastic strips. Lara bent the strips down to look through the glass, catching a glimpse of the trees and field across the street . She set her bag atop the drawers. Apart from the discomfort caused by her wet clothing, there was another pressing need she’d neglected . “Should be able to get more clothes for you to put in there,” Ronin said . Lara started, looking to the doorway. How long had he been standing there ? “In…here?” Lara slid open the top drawer . “That’s what it’s for. It’s called a dresser .” “Never seen anything as nice as this.” Each of the drawers had a little metal handle, with two on each of the top ones. “What else is supposed to go in it ?” “Just clothes, usually .” “Who has that many clothes?” she asked, bewildered. Most of the clothing she owned was on her now, and it wouldn’t fill half of one drawer. She could think of a thousand other things to store in here . “You put whatever you want in it,” Ronin said. “I’ll go get you something dry to wear.” He turned to leave . “ Wait !” He stopped and looked at her . “I need to go,” Lara said . “We have an agreement.” His
